paragraphs.text: TOGETHER AGAINST COUNTERFEITING

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
deceptive pricing
undisclosed influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: The Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance (TAC) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the proposed Digital Fairness Act. We support the Commission's ambition to tackle manipulative digital practices that distort consumer choice, enable the sale of counterfeit goods, and undermine trust in the digital single market. In particular, we welcome the focus on commercial tactics such as dark patterns, deceptive pricing, and undisclosed influencer marketing - tools increasingly used by illegal traders to promote and sell counterfeit products online.

The TAC Alliance represents over 100 companies across all sectors of the economy, supported by 20 trade associations and NGOs. We are a cross-sectoral coalition committed to confronting the growing threat of counterfeiting in the EU. Our members bring first-hand expertise in identifying and addressing the sale of counterfeit goods online and have deep knowledge of the risks these goods pose to consumer safety, legitimate businesses, and society as a whole.

In our submission, we outline key challenges linked to the persistence of counterfeit products in the digital space, the importance of enforcing existing legislation such as the Digital Services Act, and targeted recommendations to ensure that the Digital Fairness Act delivers a fairer and safer environment for consumers by contributing to the fight against counterfeiting. We look forward to supporting the Commission's work and contributing to the development of a framework that complements existing rules and strengthens consumer protection in the online marketplace.
TOGETHER AGAINST COUNTERFEITING

Recent advances in the EU's digital rulebook, notably the Digital Services Act, have strengthened the Union's capacity to address illegal content & illicit practices appearing online and improve consumer protection. Yet, important gaps remain. Consumers, especially young people and vulnerable groups, continue to face harmful online practices led by rogue actors and limited remedies when confronted with unsafe or illegal products.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative commercial practices

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act represents a welcome opportunity to address these shortcomings by targeting specific types of manipulative commercial practices not fully covered by existing legislation. Current rules do not adequately address emerging consumer protection risks, including counterfeiting.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive pricing
undisclosed advertising by influencers
interface design that obscures key information or encourages hasty purchases
social shopping

paragraphs.text: Unsafe counterfeit goods are increasingly sold online using techniques such as **deceptive pricing**, **undisclosed advertising** by influencers, new forms of e-commerce that are not adequately covered by the current rules such as **social shopping**, and **interface design** that obscures key information or encourages hasty purchases. The growing circulation of counterfeit goods highlights broader enforcement challenges across the digital landscape.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: While targeted measures are needed to address specific gaps that counterfeiters can exploit, existing frameworks must also be properly enforced. In particular, several key provisions of the Digital Services Act are not being effectively applied in practice. Addressing these enforcement gaps is essential to ensure a coherent and credible consumer protection system.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading content

paragraphs.text: The online sale of counterfeit goods remains widespread across all sectors of the economy. From pharmaceuticals and cosmetics to electronics and toys, counterfeit products frequently violate EU safety and environmental standards and pose direct risks to consumer health. Their distribution online is driven by sellers who take advantage of gaps in platform supervision obligations and exploit consumer vulnerabilities through misleading content and commercial practices.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer endorsements
misleading pricing

paragraphs.text: In 2023 alone, over 138 million counterfeit items were seized at the EU border, the highest level recorded in more than a decade. These products often appear on platforms that lack clear seller verification and are promoted to consumers through misleading pricing, imitation branding and influencer endorsements that

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: Consumers are exposed to counterfeit, unsafe and otherwise non-compliant products
organised criminal groups benefit from weak enforcement and regulatory fragmentation,
Counterfeit goods also pose serious environmental risks, as they often fail to comply with EU environmental standards.

items.topic: Product Information Transparency (safety Markings And Authenticity Guarantees)

evidence_quotes.quote: often without the information needed to make informed decisions such as safety markings, authenticity guarantees, responsible economic operators, etc.

paragraphs.text: The harms are both individual and systemic. Consumers are exposed to counterfeit, unsafe and otherwise non-compliant products, often without the information needed to make informed decisions such as safety markings, authenticity guarantees, responsible economic operators, etc. At the same time, organised criminal groups benefit from weak enforcement and regulatory fragmentation, leading to job losses, reduced public revenues - estimated at EUR 15 billion annually across Member States. Counterfeit goods also pose serious environmental risks, as they often fail to comply with EU environmental standards. This results in the release of hazardous substances during production and disposal, particularly in high-risk sectors such as pesticides, cosmetics, and toys.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: While the Digital Services Act introduced important tools to tackle illegal content and promote transparency, implementation remains inconsistent. Several key provisions, particularly those relevant to tackling counterfeiting, are not yet delivering their intended effect.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: Know Your Business Customer (KYBC): Seller verification remains weak across many platforms.
no platform is fully compliant with KYBC

paragraphs.text: Know Your Business Customer (KYBC): Seller verification remains weak across many platforms. Accuracy, reliability and completeness check of KYBC information by marketplaces is rarely demonstrated. In one case, a counterfeit listing on eBay Austria featured no meaningful business identification. A broader survey of almost 100 rightsholders confirmed that no platform is fully compliant with KYBC, with traders frequently misclassified, and identity checks often insufficient or non-transparent. These gaps are especially evident on non-EU platforms and social media channels.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: Repeat infringers: Despite clear provisions requiring platforms to act against repeat offenders, enforcement remains limited.
Listings reappear rapidly under new or modified accounts, reflecting the absence of proactive monitoring and clear deterrence.

paragraphs.text: Repeat infringers: Despite clear provisions requiring platforms to act against repeat offenders, enforcement remains limited. TAC members have reported hundreds of takedowns issued against the same seller, often without consequence. Listings reappear rapidly under new or modified accounts, reflecting the absence of proactive monitoring and clear deterrence.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: Platforms also fail to inform consumers when a product they purchased is later found to be illegal.

paragraphs.text: Consumer notification: Platforms also fail to inform consumers when a product they purchased is later found to be illegal. In most cases, no information is provided and no redress offered. This leaves consumers unaware of the risk and without any remedy, weakening trust and undermining the principle of fairness. In instances cases where consumers were informed, the information was

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: Trusted Flaggers: The Trusted Flaggers mechanism established under Article 22 of the DSA has significant potential to strengthen enforcement against illegal content. However, even when they fulfil eligibility criteria to become Trusted Flaggers, brands and IP rightsholders have not been designated as such due to the absence of clear and harmonised procedures across Member States.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: Moreover, in the e-commerce ecosystem, consumer exposure to illegal or unsafe products is enabled not only by online platforms (e.g., marketplaces), but also by a wider set of intermediaries that make listings reachable and discoverable. While the DSA acknowledges these actors as "intermediary services," key obligations, such as KYBC, are currently limited to online marketplaces. This narrow scope does not reflect the realities of modern e-commerce, where product visibility, promotion, and sales often occur outside traditional platform structures, including through social media, search engines, and other digital services that enable distance selling.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
undisclosed or misleading commercial communications, especially influencer and affiliate posts, often channel audiences to non-authorised sellers that lack authenticity signals.
deceptive pricing
opaque personalisation

paragraphs.text: There are a number of manipulative practices that directly fuel the online trade in counterfeits and that the Digital Fairness Act should address. First, dark patterns (e.g. fake scarcity and countdowns, obstructed or rushed checkouts, lookalike branding, fabricated social proof, misleading product presentation) are routinely used to nudge consumers into buying unauthorised or counterfeit goods before they can spot red flags. Second, addictive engagement and spending mechanics (infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks, relentless nudges) drive impulsive purchases of low-priced counterfeits, with the greatest impact on minors and vulnerable users. Third, undisclosed or misleading commercial communications, especially influencer and affiliate posts, often channel audiences to non-authorised sellers that lack authenticity signals. Fourth, deceptive pricing and opaque personalisation (drip fees, bogus discounts, opaque targeting) are hallmarks of counterfeit offers that mask true value, origin and risk.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: To address the risks outlined above and support the objectives of the Digital Fairness Act, we propose the following measures:

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: fight against counterfeiting

paragraphs.text: Leverage the DFA to strengthen the DSA in the fight against counterfeiting

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: intermediaries that materially contribute to the visibility, promotion, or sale of counterfeit products

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act should build on the DSA by ensuring that core obligations are fully enforced and extended to all intermediaries that materially contribute to the visibility, promotion, or sale of counterfeit products

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: formal recognition of eligible IP rightsholders as Trusted Flaggers
effective implementation of KYBC obligations across all intermediaries
application of Article 23

paragraphs.text: Ensure robust and harmonised enforcement of the DSA: Despite the entry into force of the DSA, several key provisions are not being consistently enforced. The European Commission should issue clear guidance to ensure formal recognition of eligible IP rightsholders as Trusted Flaggers, effective implementation of KYBC obligations across all intermediaries, and application of Article 23

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: TOGETHER EXAMINE COUNTERFEITING

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: notify consumers when a product they purchased is found to be illegal

paragraphs.text: against repeat infringers. Platforms should also be required to notify consumers when a product they purchased is found to be illegal, as set out in Article 32. Coordinated and consistent enforcement of these provisions is essential to reduce the circulation of counterfeit goods and uphold the credibility of the EU's digital rulebook.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: Intermediaries that facilitate the sale, visibility or promotion of goods online should be subject to a duty of care requiring them to identify and mitigate the risks of illegal activity on their services.

Platforms should be required to adopt risk-based monitoring systems, cooperate with rightsholders, and implement proportionate safeguards to ensure their services are not used to distribute illegal content or goods.

paragraphs.text: Introduce a general duty of care for all intermediaries involved in online product distribution: Intermediaries that facilitate the sale, visibility or promotion of goods online should be subject to a duty of care requiring them to identify and mitigate the risks of illegal activity on their services. This includes online marketplaces, social networks, search engines, advertising platforms, and domain name registrars. Platforms should be required to adopt risk-based monitoring systems, cooperate with rightsholders, and implement proportionate safeguards to ensure their services are not used to distribute illegal content or goods. A general duty of care would raise the baseline for accountability and help prevent harm before it occurs. DFA should extend DSA obligations to all intermediaries.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

items.topic: Delisting Of Illegal Or Harmful Websites

evidence_quotes.quote: Search engines should be required to delist URLs from both organic search results and sponsored links to prevent users from being directed to high-risk or unlawful sellers.

paragraphs.text: Introduce an enhanced notice-and-action mechanism for search engines: The DFA should require search engines to act upon receiving notice of illegal or harmful websites, including those repeatedly offering counterfeit goods or posing cybersecurity threats (e.g. phishing). Search engines should be required to delist URLs from both organic search results and sponsored links to prevent users from being directed to high-risk or unlawful sellers.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: Introduce stay-down obligations to prevent repeated exposure to illegal content

paragraphs.text: Introduce stay-down obligations to prevent repeated exposure to illegal content: Platforms and intermediaries should be required to ensure that once illegal content is removed, it cannot be re-uploaded in the same or similar form. This includes relistings under new accounts or modified descriptions. Large platforms have access to technical information - including seller IP addresses, contact details, and KYBC data - and are fully capable of implementing proactive cross-checks, AI-based fraud detection, and internal tracking systems. The DFA should close this enforcement loophole by obliging platforms to act on the information they already collect to ensure persistent illegal listings do not reappear.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: Leverage the DFA to adopt targeted measures in support of the fight against counterfeiting

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative and deceptive practices that exploit design features

paragraphs.text: The sale of counterfeit goods online is fueled by manipulative and deceptive practices that exploit design features, commercial ambiguity, and gaps in oversight. The DFA must address these tactics with targeted measures that promote transparency, accountability and consumer safety.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative interface design practices are considered illegal

paragraphs.text: Adopt European-level guidance on manipulative design practices: The Commission should publish EU-level interpretive guidance to clarify which manipulative interface design practices are considered illegal and how enforcement authorities can identify them in practice. The guidance should also draw a clear line between harmful tactics commonly exploited to promote counterfeit or unsafe

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: TOWASHINGHAM TOWNSEAFOON TIGHTYER FEDERAL AUDITORIES

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: transparent pricing

paragraphs.text: goods, and legitimate digital marketing practices such as transparent pricing, product alerts, or loyalty benefits. This would support more effective enforcement, expand regulatory capacity, and provide legal certainty to compliant businesses.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design features
gamified shopping
urgency cues
artificial engagement triggers

paragraphs.text: Address addictive design features that counterfeiters actively exploit. The DFA should restrict design features that drive compulsive or impulsive purchasing, particularly when used to promote counterfeit goods. These include gamified shopping (e.g. "spin the wheel" discount codes), urgency cues (e.g. fake low stock warnings or countdown offers), and artificial engagement triggers (e.g. fake reviews or social validation tools). Such tactics increase exposure to counterfeit products, especially among minors, and are widely used by illegal sellers to drive quick conversions before users detect red flags.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: clearly disclose paid partnerships, affiliate links, and gifted products

paragraphs.text: Prevent harmful influencer practices by setting clear rules for transparency, responsibility, and product authenticity. The DFA should require influencers to clearly disclose paid partnerships, affiliate links, and gifted products, and prohibit them from promoting illegal, unsafe, or counterfeit goods. This includes lookalike or "dupe" products that are deliberately marketed as branded alternatives, but which may be misleading, of lower quality, or dangerous. Responsibility for the promotion of illegal goods should lie with the influencer, not the brand, especially where the brand is impersonated or not party to the collaboration. Clear standards would help protect consumers and reinforce accountability across the influencer economy.

section_path: Together Against Counterfeiting Alliance contribution to the public consultation on the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: duty-of-care
stay-down obligations

items.topic: Extension Of Platform Obligations To Intermediaries Enabling Illegal Products

evidence_quotes.quote: extend core platform obligations to all intermediaries that enable the visibility and sale of illegal products

paragraphs.text: To address the growing threat of counterfeiting in the EU, the Digital Fairness Act should both strengthen the enforcement of existing rules under the DSA and introduce targeted measures to close regulatory gaps. As outlined above, the Commission should use the DFA to extend core platform obligations to all intermediaries that enable the visibility and sale of illegal products, reinforce enforcement of the DSA's key provisions, and introduce duty-of-care and stay-down obligations to prevent repeated abuse.

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design features that drive unsafe purchases
setting transparency and accountability standards for influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: In parallel, the DFA should directly tackle the manipulative practices counterfeiters rely on. This includes adopting clear EU-level guidance, addressing addictive design features that drive unsafe purchases, and setting transparency and accountability standards for influencer marketing, particularly in relation to product authenticity.

Taken together, these steps will close the gaps that counterfeiters exploit, protect vulnerable consumers and provide legal certainty for compliant traders. The TAC Alliance stands ready to support the Commission with data and technical input to help deliver a digital environment that is fair, safe and trustworthy.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
